questions.question: Explain the difference between a Max-Heap and a Min-Heap with an example each.
keyPoints: Max-Heap: Parent node has value >= children, root contains maximum element
Min-Heap: Parent node has value <= children, root contains minimum element
Example: Max-Heap [50, 30, 20, 15, 10, 8, 16] - 50 is root, children are 30,20
Example: Min-Heap [10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60] - 10 is root, all children >= 10
Both maintain complete binary tree property

questions.question: Derive the formulas for finding the parent, left child, and right child of a node at index i in a binary heap stored as an array (0-indexed).
keyPoints: Parent(i) = (i-1)/2 - subtract 1 from index, divide by 2
Left Child(i) = 2i + 1 - double index, add 1
Right Child(i) = 2i + 2 - double index, add 2
Example: For i=3: Parent=(3-1)/2=1, Left=7, Right=8
For 1-indexed: Parent=i/2, Left=2i, Right=2i+1

questions.question: Describe the steps involved in inserting a new element into a Binary Heap.
keyPoints: Step 1: Increase heap size by 1
Step 2: Place new element at the end of the array (maintains complete binary tree)
Step 3: Compare with parent; if in violation of heap property, swap
Step 4: Repeat Step 3 until heap property is satisfied or root is reached
Time complexity: O(log n) - worst case element rises to root

questions.question: What is Heapify operation? Explain the sift-down process with an example.
keyPoints: Heapify: Process to restore heap property after removal or building
Sift-down (for max-heap): Compare node with its two children
Find larger child; if node < larger child, swap them
Repeat until node >= both children or reaches leaf level
Example: Heapify at root [10, 30, 20, 15, 25] → after sift-down: [30, 25, 20, 15, 10]
Time complexity: O(log n), depends on height of node

questions.question: Explain the Build-Heap operation. Why is it more efficient to start heapifying from index floor(n/2)?
keyPoints: Build-Heap converts unsorted array into valid heap
Start from index floor(n/2) - 1 down to 0
Apply heapify at each index
Why floor(n/2): Elements at indices >= floor(n/2) are leaf nodes (have no children)
Leaf nodes already satisfy heap property trivially
Only non-leaf nodes need heapify - there are exactly floor(n/2) of them
Total time complexity: O(n) - not O(n log n) as commonly assumed

questions.question: Explain how Heap Sort works. What are its time and space complexities?
keyPoints: Step 1: Build max-heap from unsorted array (O(n))
Step 2: Repeatedly extract max (root), place at end of array
Step 3: Reduce heap size by 1, restore heap property
Step 4: Repeat until heap is empty
Time Complexity: O(n log n) for all cases (best, average, worst)
Space Complexity: O(1) - in-place sorting
Advantage over quicksort: Guaranteed O(n log n), no worst-case O(n²)

questions.question: Compare Binary Heap with Binary Search Tree (BST) for implementing a Priority Queue.
keyPoints: Binary Heap: Insert O(log n), Extract-Max O(log n), Peek O(1), Space O(n) array
BST (balanced): Insert O(log n), Extract-Max O(log n), Peek O(1), Space O(n) nodes
BST (unbalanced): Worst case O(n) for operations
Heap advantage: Guaranteed O(log n), uses contiguous array memory
BST advantage: Can perform ordered traversal, find kth element easily
Heap is preferred for priority queue due to guaranteed performance and memory efficiency

questions.question: Write an algorithm for Extract-Max operation in a Max-Heap. Trace through the algorithm step by step for the heap: [50, 30, 40, 20, 25, 35, 10]. Show the state of the heap after each swap.
keyPoints: Algorithm steps: 1) Store root value (max) 2) Replace root with last element 3) Reduce heap size 4) Apply heapify from root
Detailed trace showing each comparison and swap
Initial heap as array: [50, 30, 40, 20, 25, 35, 10]
After removing 50 and replacing with 10: [10, 30, 40, 20, 25, 35]
Heapify step 1: Compare 10 with children 30 and 40, swap with 40: [40, 30, 10, 20, 25, 35]
Heapify step 2: Compare 10 with children 20 and 25, swap with 25: [40, 30, 35, 20, 25, 10]
Final heap: [40, 30, 35, 20, 25, 10], extracted max = 50
Time complexity: O(log n)

questions.question: Sort the array [25, 57, 48, 37, 12, 92, 33] using Heap Sort. Show all steps: building the heap, each extraction, and the final sorted array.
keyPoints: Build max-heap from array using heapify from bottom-up
Step-by-step heap building: apply heapify at indices 2, 1, 0
Intermediate heap states after each heapify
Extract-Max steps: swap root with last, reduce size, heapify
Show array after each extraction
Final sorted array in ascending order: [12, 25, 33, 37, 48, 57, 92]
Total time: O(n log n), space: O(1)

questions.question: Design a priority queue using Binary Min-Heap. Write functions for: (a) Insert (b) Extract-Min (c) Peek (d) Decrease-Key. Also analyze their time complexities.
keyPoints: Insert: Place at end, sift-up - O(log n)
Extract-Min: Replace root with last, heapify root - O(log n)
Peek: Return root element - O(1)
Decrease-Key: Find element, decrease value, sift-up - O(log n)
All operations maintain heap property
Array implementation using 0-indexed formulas
Corner cases: empty heap, single element, decrease-key at root

questions.question: Prove that building a heap from n elements takes O(n) time, not O(n log n), using the analysis of heapify operations.
keyPoints: At height h from bottom, there are at most ceil(n/2^(h+1)) nodes
Each node at height h requires at most h comparisons/swaps
Total work = sum over all nodes of (height of node)
This sum equals: 0*ceil(n/2) + 1*ceil(n/4) + 2*ceil(n/8) + ... + h_max*1
This forms a convergent series = O(n)
More intuitive: Most nodes are near bottom (small height), few at top
Only root has height log n, half have height 1, quarter have height 2, etc.
